speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much and good afternoon everybody. Sorry for the delay. We had some technical challenges
here in Chennai from where we're taking the call. Today – first of all, of course, I'm Kim Junge Andersen, the CFO
and with me here I have the CEO, Jens Birgersson, and today we will present to you the First Half Result and
then afterwards we go into Q&A.
Before we start, I'd just like to be remind you on slide number 2, the forward-looking statement and please be
aware that this presentation contains uncertainties.
And with this we can quickly go into the presentation on slide number 3.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Good morning. Good day, everyone. It's Jens here. Kim and I and the whole board of dire of ROCKWOOL,
including my successor, Jes Munk, we are in India, in Chennai. And the reason we are here doing a board
meeting – about 90 kilometers southwest of Chennai, which was previously called Madras, we have bought a
piece of land and we have approved a factory project for our second factory in India, where we have had quite a
successful from last couple of years filling up our first factory. So, that's the reason we are calling from here, and I
really can't blame India for the technical problems. Everything works here, it's some other issue that's creating
problems here.
So, if we move to slide 3, the H1 results, as you know I normally don't comment on half year very much but in the
contrast between H1 and Q2, it's a little bit interesting. So, in H1 we saw 8%, quite broad-based growth as we
have seen in previous probably in the last 1.5 year is the commercial side of the business going. We don't see
much growth on residential, rather the opposite. So the residential is still trouble. But we are doing fine on the
commercial side of things.
If we look at result year-to-date, EBIT margin of 17.7%, and that has been achieved on more or less neutral
pricing. We have a fraction of the price reduction. There is a mix of market, in some markets the price is going up.
For example, North America and parts of Asia. And there are those where the prices just hold and some where
they are down, but we're kind of monitoring our market share and navigating that. So overall, we are very happy
with the first half year.
Moving on to slide 4, second quarter highlights. Comparable differs a little bit. So, Q2 wasn't very strong last year.
Q1 wasn't strong either last year. So, I would say we had a really broad-based growth, and record growth in North
America. I don't think, we ever had a bigger growth number and higher sales, that goes for Canada and the US.
Overall sales [indiscernible] (00:03:40) good to see that we crossed €1 billion. I'm not sure if that's the first time,
but very, very strong quarter. And then on the EBIT margin, we reached 18.7% and that includes €7 million for
creating a provision for donations. And just to sum up, [ph] we had in Q-on-Q or plus Q2 is (00:04:07) little bit
more than €13 million that we have donated.
Let's move to slide 5. Just on – I'm not really going to comment this, but if you look at up on the right, the Systems
division's growth, you see minus 1%. We did divest Charles Wille, so on like-for-like numbers, Systems division
have 2 percentage point better growth, that's shown there.
If we now shift to slide 6, we see Q2. And what's pleasing here is to see that here, obviously, there's the Insulation
business is growing very, very much, it's a super good quarter. But maybe from my perspective even more
pleasing is that Systems division has gained a little bit of momentum. So, if we add back the 2% lost top line for
Charles Wille, we have about 5% on a comparable basis. So, we are looking now in the coming quarters whether
the growth will increase a little bit in the Systems division. The main driver for the Systems division growth is
Rockpanel and Grodan that have had a very strong development.
If we turn to slide 7, the regional sales development starting with Europe, it's a very scattered picture in Western
Europe. We have some countries growing 20%, 30%. We have Sweden growing [ph] pricingly (00:06:12) quite
well, Germany, single digit growth, healthy growth [ph] back (00:06:18) turning the corner, although we don't see
Germany on the residential side turning up at all, but that's on the commercial side. Then, we have countries like
Spain, France, UK that are around the zero or slightly negative. So, quite a wide spread on that.
Moving to Eastern Europe and Russia, you see several countries we had very solid double digit growth numbers
and one or two of the small amounts are negative. And we are not listed here that Russia had a very good
quarter, but so did Poland and so did, for example, Ukraine. We are small in Ukraine, but we grew some 40% in
Ukraine.
Moving on to North America, Asia and others. A quick summary of the whole thing that is that China is negative,
US and Canada is on record growth, and Southeast Asia, including Japan very good growth, so Thailand,
Malaysia, Indonesia and where we are now, India, on very good growth. We are getting closer to a point where
we can't sale more. But it's nice to see that we are growing out here and also in North America.
Let's go to profitability on slide 8. Margin has increased quite a lot if you look there on the right hand diagram,
EBITDA margin up to 25%, EBIT debt margin now 18.7%, which is a lot higher than a year back. So, the trend
upwards have been healthy. We have listed some of the factors. Obviously, volume growth, stable sales prices,
input costs have kept stable. We see now maybe inflation coming back a little bit, we have had some favorable
factors coming in, obviously compare on the inflation. But we see also very many materials kind of kicking up in
normal inflation.
One item here that is worth noting, if you look at the head count, we are about [ph] 100 (00:08:43) employees, so
higher in the head count compared to a year back and that is where the 10% growth. So obviously, we had a
tremendous productivity improvement there, almost 9%, 10% productivity improvement and that gives a nice
overabsorption where we – fixed cost on the factory overheads and all the rest, and you'll see that flow through to
the bottom line. So it's nice to see that, that scalability is there in the business.
I'm also quite happy with [indiscernible] (00:09:19) ramp up in the factories where for example in North America
we have to step up capacity very quickly. And also some European countries step up, Germany, for example,
increased the shifts, but we have been able to do that relatively quickly and that has helped us in supporting this
growth.
If we move to slide 9, here I only want to comment on margins in Q2 2024. And I guess I spoke about the 5% like-
for-like growth of Systems division. Here I'm quite pleased with that Systems division is up around 15% margin
again. Of course, Insulation, that margin is doing very well and I also want to maybe clarify that the whole Ukraine
donation has been put on Insulation, so that you are aware of that.
Look into the investment activities, nothing of worth, you recognized most of these things. But we have done a
small acquisition in Vietnam. We have signed and we are very close to close, I think four to six weeks. And just to
underline that we are here with the board in Asia, we are since – since a while have been working here in Asia to
step up our efforts, so I am – it's a very small acquisition, but yet an important bridgehead into Vietnam and many
of us might not know that even a country like Vietnam has 100 million people and a very great outlook at the
moment. So, I am happy that we could get that one because, again, it's clearly growth markets and it's good to
have [indiscernible] (00:11:35) stone wool factory in the country. We're also working on some other targets in the
region. There's not a lot around, but we are scouting.
Move on to slide 11. Net working capital percentage 11.5%, down compared to a year back from 13.4%, nothing
special with that, it's nothing we really need to focus a lot on, it sits inherent in the business, but we don't tie up
too much net working capital. If we had [indiscernible] (00:12:10) that number, due to the volume growth we
perhaps would have liked to have a little bit more finished goods inventory, we have to sell a little bit more than
we wanted to and we [indiscernible] (00:12:23) now maybe to bring that inventory up in the coming quarters.
And then the last bullet there the net cash position, around €200 million, net cash positive, and that's after the
dividend and it's after the share buyback. We are on track. We had about €160 million buyback planned for this
year and we are pacing relatively well on the curve on that front. So, good cash in spite of high investment
obviously with the earnings level we have, now we have a lot of cash in the company. So, good to see that.
Slide 12, as we normally do – in the half-year report, we normally include one or two slides on sustainability, we
have done that also here. There's not much drama about that. We have the five sustainability goals with a time
horizon to 2030. We're on track or ahead on all of those.
Now just pull out a couple of data points here – okay, I'll take it from my memory. So, if you look first on the CO2
emission intensity, this is the goal we defined before science-based target existed, and as an engineer, that's a
good metric. Here we have achieved with the index 2015, 100, in terms of tonne CO2 per produced tonne stone
wool. We had the reference level 2015 of 100 and now inherently in the business we have reached the index 84,
so that's 16 points, so 16% down in tonne produced through productivity improvements, conversions, green
energy and some other actions. And we will see this number now continue down over the coming years because
we are investing and we are taking actions, and in the annual report and the sustainability report, you will see a
number, the [indiscernible] (00:14:53) so that's progressing well. And, you know the green investment on the
CapEx slide, you see [ph] what type of money (00:15:01) we put into that.
We are also working now, as we said, on our energy supplies and the contracting around that to see if we can get
out of this extreme volatility the next round. We have an energy crisis and something like that, so that – the effort
is ongoing.
Science-based target that we also have gone for targets about CO2 equivalent emissions in absolute terms,
excluding acquisitions. We have a goal for that too. And it's quite pleasing to see that when the business is
growing volume and top line double digit that our CO2 emissions only increased with 6% on a more than 10%
volume and sales growth. So that's good – that shows that, that kind of emissions per tonne is working. It also
shows the – underlines the challenge we have on 2030 and onwards of really, really convert the whole company
more or less into electrical melting driven by green energy.
So, we need – it's the really challenging target, but we are working on and at this stage we don't feel that it's
impossible. We have the technologies. We have the investment plan. But then, of course, the more we grow, the
more challenging that's going to become. So at the moment, I would say the long-term growth outlook is quite
positive. So, it's a challenging goal, but we are working, and we can see that the actions we have taken are good.
On the Rockcycle, goal here is [indiscernible] (00:16:56) reclaimed waste schemes, but then take back our own
products and upcycle them or recycle them. We have added now also Poland to the countries where we offer this
service and we have now 22 countries in the scheme and the goal is 30 by 2030. So, we are well ahead of that.
We will beat that goal within really – much before 2030.
And then on safety goal, no serious accidents and no fatalities in the first half year and it's what we like to see and
that's how we need to perform all the time. But as you know we have a big, big operation with many factories, and
these factories since – things can happen, but we are working very seriously on that, and it's good that we have a
good first half year.
Going to the outlook, slide 14. Going forward on sales, we haven't seen any change or any dramatic
developments in the market, but we are stepping in a couple of quarters where you have more challenging
comparable. So on the top line that mid-single digit percentage growth seems like a good outlook still, although
we are ahead of that until now. But that's because [indiscernible] (00:18:39) as we see it now, we see the
business pretty much continue, that's good. On the EBIT margin, around 17%, also there we are a little bit ahead,
but then as you know we have the December effect, could go either way. We also have the summer in France,
profitable business for us, impact a bit, but we feel comfortable with that outlook and on the investment, nothing
really to report on that, we are on track to invest what we've planned to invest for the year.
Should stay though on the investment slide, we have had one setback, our new plant in France, we were
surprised, I must say that we got a negative verdict on one of the lawsuits. So we are regrouping on that, we have
not at all given up, the new factory in France, but we need to look at that and see how we keep pushing the plant
in France. If not, we should be able to start building it now but this was a setback, and we can expect a further
delay, possibly we'll move the resources and accelerate another project. We have a number of projects start, but
we have absolutely not given up on the French plant, we will keep working through that. Over to questions.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you. Yeah, two questions from my side. I'll do them one by one. The first question goes to inflation. Jens,
you mentioned that you do see – it sounded like a small inflation. Does that mean you're going to raise prices or
will you have to absorb that in your P&L, so to speak? That will be the first one.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Yeah. So, Claus, thanks for introducing yourself. We don't have the normal screen, but we can see who
asked the question. I know who you are, so great if you can introduce yourself like that so we know. No, I – yeah,
I see – I mean, the inflation doesn't go away. We had energy benefit and some other benefits, but we have
several markets where we foresee that we will do a small drumbeat price increase to cover that inflation. There
could be competitive dynamics in some places where we might not do that. But the overall direction is that we are
ready to do a small price increase to pass on the inflationary pressures as far as we can.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. And then leading to the next question, which is – your implicit second half guide, and you did touch upon
this in your slides. But implicitly, you guided for around 15% EBIT margin in the second half, so down versus first
half, also if we adjust for the Ukraine donation. Is this only due to France and December sale, as you said, or is it
also the inflation impact? You'd previously said it's not easy to raise prices in all markets, so you had to be more
specific about these price increases.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Yeah. I saw your – headline of your initial comment, and I would say we are not – you have seen some
companies that have released their earnings report now and that have seen decline in top line and that is quite a
mixed picture in different related sectors. So, we haven't seen any of that. So, we are not pessimistic. We are not
pessimistic on the prices. I think we can do most of that. Yes, we believe there is inflationary pressures, but we
have the ambition to pass that on. So that's the outlook.
And then we did upgrade – when was it – 18th of July to 17%. We felt to – to make an assessment now is not
prudent. We feel comfortable with the 17%. We obviously have no ambition to reduce the profitability, but we
acknowledge the December effect and the France effect as we always do. So, I will leave that to my successor to
kind of nail that outlook in his maybe first earnings call.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
[indiscernible] (00:24:03) and you know that, Claus, we're coming close and close to the autumn period, where
many of our markets will negotiate 2025 prices. There's always a little bit of the timing there.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So, I wouldn't do straight the mathematics and say we've concluded that this and that, we'll
come back on that forecast little bit deeper into those discussions. And I also say I – we feel comfortable with the
17% and the next time we will know more.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Nordea Bank Abp (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text: Fair enough. Thank you so much for the answers.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Thanks.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, ABG Sundal Collier ASA (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Great. It's Peter from ABG. Thanks for taking my questions. I have two. The first one is on France. The
MaPrimeRénov program has noted an increase in applications following their return to [indiscernible] (00:25:13)
application procedures, and I guess that these applications will probably not hit the market or realized in the
second half. Is this something that you have taken into account in your guidance? Because I know that in France
the renovation program has been a very important driver for ROCKWOOL for the past? That would be the first
question.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. It's accounted for. We have a good profitable business and France is slightly in the negative territory in the
quarter and we haven't factored in any hopes and dreams from the scheme. So, we have a good conservative or
realistic forecast and that's included.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, ABG Sundal Collier ASA (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. Good. And then just going back to the guidance, because your wording on pricing is essentially the same
as you had – you did in Q1 and Q4. Also with respect – and we have sort of the usual thing about the December
impact, that's also something that you typically iterate around this time of year.
A
Jens Birgersson
President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
Yeah.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, ABG Sundal Collier ASA (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text: Is there anything that is sort of different this time that it has been...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: No.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, ABG Sundal Collier ASA (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Let's say...
[indiscernible] (00:26:32)

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
No, no, it's very much – it's very much business as usual. We drive a very strict pricing process in the company,
we stay on top of the markets and we try to take that very serious. And there is some – and the dynamics out
there that is a different to another year, of course – and we don't have the situation we had in the autumn 2022
with the hyperinflation and all this. So, I'll say business as usual and there is a slight inflation that we've been
trying to go after it, and it's not – no drama around the whole thing. But then of course, you have some market,
some segments [indiscernible] (00:27:16) we worked on all that, but so far so good.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, ABG Sundal Collier ASA (Denmark)
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you for taking my questions, and also I'd like to say thanks for your effort and what you've done at
ROCKWOOL. I think you've set [ph] – you and Kim and the team (00:27:30) set a very high, let's say, benchmark
for the incoming CEO, so good luck to him as well.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Thanks, Peter.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much, Peter. We have the same team in place and we got a very strong pair of hands taking over
the business. So, I'm very optimistic about the future. Thanks. Next?
Operator: Your next question will come from Arnaud Lehmann with Bank of America. Please go ahead.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much. My name is Arnaud Lehmann from Bank of America. My first question is I guess related to
pricing margin and returns, staying on that topic. You indeed have done very well on margins and that drove an
improvement in ROIC, which you published, I think 23% is the printed number in the H1 report over the last – I
think that's 12 months rolling beta, which compared to 15% last year. I appreciate there's an element of, let's say,
inflation in CapEx and the cost of adding new plants has gone up. But on the other hand, I don't believe you have
a broad based plants to increase capacity. It's more of a selective process. So, I guess my question is, beyond
pricing and margin levels, do you believe that these sort of returns are sustainable going forward? Or is there a
possibility for some sort of normalization over time?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. I would like to – I will give just a brief flavor on that reflection, Arnaud, and then Kim should comment it
because he continues. My view on that is that we are not – we want to have 15% or above, but the biggest job as
the CEO – or what I've seen is that for me it's not so important if it's 23% or 16% or whatever. It's important that
we keep supporting the volume growth and at the end in our industry, if you have the capacity, you can deliver the
volume.
So, from my perspective, I think, yes, we want to have a good cash generation with a good margin, but we would
like to invest more. Sometimes we get delays like now in France, the plant, we would really like to build that, and
that would be a burden for quite a while such a big plant on the return on capital employed. And quite frankly, I'd
rather have a couple of percentage point lower return on capital employed and get that plant built because that's
better for the long term for this business. So, that's how we look at it. So, I think that now you have a combination
of the – some projects are hanging and yes, we are building plant in India, plant in Romania, we are building one
in Sweden...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: In US.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
In the US. We are building, it's not just selected, we are building and we're going to step up CapEx, but it's quite
hard to build. So, it's not a goal in itself for me to keep it to 2023. But I should let Kim...
[indiscernible] (00:30:55)

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Senior Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
No. But the short term, 2023 or 2024 – close to 2024 compared to 15% last year, last year was a bit abnormally
low because that was impacted by the 2022 downturn in performance. And maybe this year is a bit abnormally
high since we have relatively low working capital at the beginning of the year. So I think it's a – I think a
normalization would be around 20s. But as Jens said, it's not something that we steer to watch necessarily on the
short term ROICs, we do the 15%-plus target setting internally and then we go for this, but we will step up
investment in the coming years. So, Jes and myself will maybe not be able to present 24% ROIC short term. In
the short term that's where the numbers are.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America
speaker_turns.text:
Very good. Thank you. Thank you for that. My second question, if that's okay, is regarding Russia. I mean, no
change as far as I can tell in terms of the way you're thinking about the ownership of these assets for
ROCKWOOL. Should we expect the change with a new management in the future, I guess, maybe [indiscernible]
(00:32:10).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
We have worked this through in detail. My successor is still until the 1st of September, a board member. He is
part of it. And, we haven't seen the external environment on the reasoning for not continue what we do. We
haven't seen anything change, and we see the companies who take the other route, it impacts very negatively. So
we see no reason to change and there is no intention to change the strategy because of the a CEO of the
company, and we are all aligned on this.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Bank of America
speaker_turns.text: Thank you very much.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Yes. Good afternoon. So, two question on my side as well. First, could you comment a little bit about the pricing
trends and the volume trend that you've seen in July and August? Is it fair to assume that there is no change in
pricing, or have announced some additional price increase? I think in the US there were some price increases.
And on the volume, have you seen any improvement or deterioration versus second quarter?
And then my second question would be on the new entrants. Like [indiscernible] (00:33:45) relatively high today,
do you see a risk for new companies to enter the European or North American mineral wool industry or do you
think it's too hard like because of a regulation like – and they could face the same issue that you are facing in
France.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. So, in France, we see some pricing pressure in the residential segment [indiscernible] (00:34:12) activity is
relatively low. We haven't seen much of a change. I mean, it's around zero or slightly negative – slightly up,
nothing dramatic. And I think we need some, I don't know, governmental programs taking action to get that up.
Overall, in Europe, on the GDP side of things, we probably haven't seen a case for a broad-based upturn. It has
weakened a little bit lately in Europe. So, France sits around zero or slightly negative. Nothing really dramatic on
price, maybe some segments a bit down but other segments we can increase the prices. So that's on that side.
And then, price increases, there are some market where we – there is no issue whatsoever with price. For
example, the US, we just launched 8% increase and that's in line with what happening in the market on glass
wool and stone wool, that's moving.
The last question, just to make sure I understood, you asked me how difficult it is to enter the stone wool
business, was that the question?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Do you see a risk, because the margin today are much higher than they were [ph] 10 weeks (00:35:29)
ago?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text: Yeah. Definitely yeah.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
Do you see a risk that you will see new player coming into the market?
[indiscernible] (00:35:31)

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Obviously, there are people that look at stone wool now, and they go, this is the fantastic business, let's
enter this, and you have 18% EBIT margin and the rest. And I think having a single plant versus our quite big
machine now with a lot of economics of scale and also considering how difficult it is to start up a plant and run a
really, really productive outfit. And I think you will see some entrants, but I must say there hasn't been an entrant
for a long time that come even close to our margins, because it's very, very difficult to run a small network or just
one plant without brand and without the service level and all the rest.
So, I'm not particularly worried about new entrants, but yes, some people will announce new plants and we'll deal
with that when that comes. But the trend historically has been that [indiscernible] (00:36:52) are announced and
very much delayed because it's also very, very difficult to build the plants. You need to be very persistent in some
geographies to get your plant build in today's world. And I think their scale is also important because we have the
means and the persistence to just keep working it until we can build our plants. So it's not – it can be very
challenging to get all the permits, when you get the permits and then you have [indiscernible] (00:37:22) whatever
as it is in many industries in Europe [indiscernible] (00:37:27).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, On Field Investment Research LLP
speaker_turns.text:
And maybe just very last point which is [indiscernible] (00:37:32) not a question but more a comment about the
fact that you've delivered such a greater growth for the company. I think it's quite impressive what you've been
able to do in terms of margin and productivity. So, just wanted to wish you the best of luck for your future
endeavor. Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, ROCKWOOL A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you very much, Yassine. And you mentioned an aspect there that is an important one to us that we don't
talk about too much, and that is our productivity, that economics of scale of the business. That's an underlying big
advantage of ROCKWOOL that we have in the culture and that's a strong point. But, thank you for those words.
